content.text: Logging and Structured Events in Python Data Pipelines
Observability is what lets you understand what your system is actually doing — not just whether it's running, but

it's running, where it slows down, what it processed, and why it failed. In data pipelines, where a single run might touch millions of records across dozens of steps, having rich, queryable logs isn't a luxury — it's the foundation of reliable operation.
is the practice of recording events that happen during program execution. A
(or structured log) is a log entry represented as a machine-readable data structure — typically a JSON object — rather than a plain English sentence.
2024-01-15 10:23:45 INFO Processed 1500 records from orders table
Both convey the same fact, but only the second is trivially filterable, aggregatable, alertable, and dashboardable by any log management system.

Data pipelines are opaque by nature. They run in the background, often unattended, processing data that flows through multiple stages. When something goes wrong — and in production, something always does — you need to answer questions like:
Which batch failed, and at exactly which record?
How long did each stage take compared to yesterday?
Did the failure affect downstream consumers?
Was the issue data quality or infrastructure?

Without structured logging, answering these requires either reading raw text logs (slow, error-prone) or re-running the pipeline to reproduce the issue (dangerous). Structured events turn your logs into a queryable event store, making these questions answerable in seconds.
— Structured fields can be filtered precisely. "Show me all runs where
is a trivial query in any log system.
— You can compute average latency per stage, total throughput per day, or error rate per data source across thousands of runs automatically.
— Alert systems like PagerDuty or Grafana can trigger on specific field values (e.g.,
) rather than fragile regex pattern matching on text strings.
field lets you trace a single pipeline execution through every system it touches — your pipeline logs, database logs, and external API logs all connected.
— Finding the root cause of a failure drops from hours to minutes when you can instantly retrieve the exact context of any event.

— Structured logs are more verbose than plain text. A JSON object with 10 fields for every record processed in a 10-million-record pipeline generates significant storage and I/O overhead. Log sampling and level filtering are essential.
— Serializing Python dicts to JSON on every log call has a small but nonzero CPU cost. In tight inner loops processing millions of records, this matters.
calls need to adopt a new discipline of thinking in events and fields rather than sentences.
— Structured logs become inconsistent when different parts of the codebase add fields with different names or types for the same concept (
module has a well-designed architecture that is often underused. Understanding it properly lets you build sophisticated observability without third-party dependencies.
). Loggers are named in a hierarchy using dot notation:
, and log records propagate upward through the hierarchy unless propagation is disabled.
writes to files that rotate at a size limit. Multiple handlers can be attached to a single logger.
a log record looks. This is where the structure lives. A custom formatter can serialize the record as JSON.
— An optional component that can accept or reject records before they reach a handler, based on any arbitrary logic.
— The internal Python object created for every log call. It carries the message, level, timestamp, filename, line number, and any

root logger
  └── myapp
        └── myapp.pipeline
               ├── myapp.pipeline.extract
               ├── myapp.pipeline.transform
               └── myapp.pipeline.load
logs a record, it travels up to

, then the root logger. Any handler attached at any level in the chain will receive it. This means you can configure one handler at the root and have all loggers in your entire application use it automatically — a very clean pattern.
Architecture of a Structured Logging System

[ Your Pipeline Code ]
        │
        │  logger.info("batch_processed", extra={...})
        ▼
[ Logger (named, hierarchical) ]
        │
        │  propagates upward
        ▼
[ Root Logger + Handler ]
        │
        ├─► StreamHandler ──► JSON Formatter ──► stdout
        │
        └─► FileHandler ──────► JSON Formatter ──► logs/pipeline.jsonl
                                                         │
                                              (picked up by log shipper)
                                                         │
                                                         ▼
                                             [ Elasticsearch / Loki /
                                               CloudWatch / Datadog ]
                                                         │
                                                         ▼
                                              [ Grafana / Kibana /
                                                Alerting System ]
The key insight is that your code only talks to a
. The infrastructure question of where logs go and how they're stored is completely decoupled and handled by configuration.
module is powerful but awkward to use for structured output.
is the standard library for structured logging in Python. It wraps the built-in module and makes structured events the primary API.
This configuration should run exactly once, at the top of your

import logging
import structlog
import sys

def configure_logging(log_level: str = "INFO", json_output: bool = True):
    """
    Configure structlog for structured JSON output in production,
    or human-readable colored output in development.
    """
    shared_processors = [
        # Adds log level name to the event dict
        structlog.stdlib.add_log_level,
        # Adds the logger name to the event dict
        structlog.stdlib.add_logger_name,
        # Adds ISO 8601 timestamp
        structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt="iso"),
        # Formats exceptions with tracebacks
        structlog.processors.format_exc_info,
        # Renders stack traces if stack_info=True is passed
        structlog.processors.StackInfoRenderer(),
    ]

    if json_output:
        # Production: output each event as a single JSON line
        renderer = structlog.processors.JSONRenderer()
    else:
        # Development: colorized, human-readable key=value output
        renderer = structlog.dev.ConsoleRenderer()

    structlog.configure(
        processors=shared_processors + [renderer],
        wrapper_class=structlog.stdlib.BoundLogger,
        context_var=structlog.contextvars.merge_contextvars,
        logger_factory=structlog.stdlib.LoggerFactory(),
        cache_logger_on_first_use=True,
    )

    # Also configure the stdlib logging module so any libraries
    # that use stdlib logging still get captured and formatted
    logging.basicConfig(
        format="%(message)s",
        stream=sys.stdout,
        level=getattr(logging, log_level.upper()),
    )
Step 3: Use Context Binding — The Most Important Pattern
. You bind fields to a logger once, and every subsequent log call from that logger automatically includes those fields. This is how you ensure that
appear in every log event from a particular execution without passing them manually to every call.

import structlog
import uuid

log = structlog.get_logger()

def run_pipeline(pipeline_name: str, source_table: str):
    # Create a run-scoped logger with fields bound for the entire run
    run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    run_log = log.bind(
        pipeline=pipeline_name,
        run_id=run_id,
        source_table=source_table,
    )

    run_log.info("pipeline_started")

    try:
        records = extract(run_log, source_table)
        transformed = transform(run_log, records)
        load(run_log, transformed)
        run_log.info("pipeline_completed", total_records=len(records))
    except Exception as e:
        run_log.error("pipeline_failed", error=str(e), exc_info=True)
        raise
as a parameter. Inside each function, they can bind additional context (e.g.,
) and every log call in that function automatically carries
Step 4: Log Events at Stage Boundaries

import time
import structlog
from contextlib import contextmanager
from typing import Iterator

@contextmanager
def log_stage(logger: structlog.BoundLogger, stage: str) -> Iterator[structlog.BoundLogger]:
    """
    Context manager that logs stage entry/exit with timing,
    and binds the stage name to all logs within the block.
    """
    stage_log = logger.bind(stage=stage)
    stage_log.info("stage_started")
    start = time.perf_counter()
    try:
        yield stage_log
        duration_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        stage_log.info("stage_completed", duration_ms=round(duration_ms, 2))
    except Exception as e:
        duration_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        stage_log.error(
            "stage_failed",
            duration_ms=round(duration_ms, 2),
            error_type=type(e).__name__,
            error=str(e),
            exc_info=True,
        )
        raise


def extract(logger, source_table: str) -> list[dict]:
    with log_stage(logger, "extract") as log:
        # Simulate extraction
        records = fetch_from_db(source_table)
        log.info("records_fetched", record_count=len(records))
        return records


def transform(logger, records: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    with log_stage(logger, "transform") as log:
        results = []
        failed = 0
        for i, record in enumerate(records):
            try:
                results.append(apply_transformations(record))
            except ValueError as e:
                failed += 1
                log.warning(
                    "record_transform_failed",
                    record_index=i,
                    record_id=record.get("id"),
                    error=str(e),
                )
        log.info(
            "transform_summary",
            total=len(records),
            succeeded=len(results),
            failed=failed,
        )
        return results
Step 5: Use Context Variables for Request/Run Scope

, which means you can set fields that automatically appear in all log calls anywhere in the current async task or thread — without passing the logger explicitly. This is extremely useful in FastAPI or async pipelines.

import structlog

# At the start of a pipeline run (e.g., in a FastAPI endpoint or Celery task):
structlog.contextvars.clear_contextvars()
structlog.contextvars.bind_contextvars(
    run_id=run_id,
    pipeline="orders_etl",
    environment="production",
)

# Now ANY logger in ANY module will include these fields automatically:
log = structlog.get_logger()
log.info("doing_something")
# Output: {"event": "doing_something", "run_id": "abc123", "pipeline": "orders_etl", ...}
Step 6: Handle Sampling for High-Volume Inner Loops
Logging every record in a million-record batch is impractical. Use sampling or periodic logging instead.

def transform_records(logger, records: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    results = []
    log_every_n = max(1, len(records) // 20)  # Log ~20 progress events

    for i, record in enumerate(records):
        result = apply_transformations(record)
        results.append(result)

        # Periodic progress log
        if (i + 1) % log_every_n == 0 or (i + 1) == len(records):
            logger.info(
                "transform_progress",
                processed=i + 1,
                total=len(records),
                pct_complete=round(((i + 1) / len(records)) * 100, 1),
            )

    return results

# pipeline.py
import uuid
import time
import structlog
import logging
import sys
from contextlib import contextmanager

# --- Configuration ---

def configure_logging():
    structlog.configure(
        processors=[
            structlog.contextvars.merge_contextvars,
            structlog.stdlib.add_log_level,
            structlog.stdlib.add_logger_name,
            structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt="iso"),
            structlog.processors.format_exc_info,
            structlog.processors.JSONRenderer(),
        ],
        logger_factory=structlog.stdlib.LoggerFactory(),
        cache_logger_on_first_use=True,
    )
    logging.basicConfig(format="%(message)s", stream=sys.stdout, level=logging.INFO)

# --- Utilities ---

@contextmanager
def timed_stage(logger, stage_name: str):
    bound = logger.bind(stage=stage_name)
    bound.info("stage_started")
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    try:
        yield bound
        elapsed = round((time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000, 2)
        bound.info("stage_completed", duration_ms=elapsed)
    except Exception:
        elapsed = round((time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000, 2)
        bound.error("stage_failed", duration_ms=elapsed, exc_info=True)
        raise

# --- Fake Data Functions ---

def fetch_records(table: str) -> list[dict]:
    return [{"id": i, "value": i * 10, "tag": "A" if i % 2 == 0 else "B"} for i in range(1, 1001)]

def transform_record(record: dict) -> dict:
    if record["value"] < 0:
        raise ValueError(f"Negative value: {record['value']}")
    return {**record, "value_doubled": record["value"] * 2}

def write_records(records: list[dict], destination: str):
    pass  # Simulate write

# --- Pipeline Stages ---

def run_extract(log, table: str) -> list[dict]:
    with timed_stage(log, "extract") as slog:
        records = fetch_records(table)
        slog.info("extract_complete", record_count=len(records), source_table=table)
        return records

def run_transform(log, records: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    with timed_stage(log, "transform") as slog:
        success, failed = [], 0
        for record in records:
            try:
                success.append(transform_record(record))
            except ValueError as e:
                failed += 1
                slog.warning("record_skipped", record_id=record["id"], reason=str(e))

        slog.info(
            "transform_complete",
            succeeded=len(success),
            failed=failed,
            failure_rate=round(failed / len(records), 4) if records else 0,
        )
        return success

def run_load(log, records: list[dict], destination: str):
    with timed_stage(log, "load") as slog:
        write_records(records, destination)
        slog.info("load_complete", record_count=len(records), destination=destination)

# --- Main Entrypoint ---

def run_pipeline(source_table: str, destination: str):
    configure_logging()

    run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    structlog.contextvars.clear_contextvars()
    structlog.contextvars.bind_contextvars(
        run_id=run_id,
        pipeline="daily_etl",
        environment="production",
    )

    log = structlog.get_logger()
    log.info("pipeline_started", source=source_table, destination=destination)
    t0 = time.perf_counter()

    try:
        records = run_extract(log, source_table)
        transformed = run_transform(log, records)
        run_load(log, transformed, destination)

        total_ms = round((time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000, 2)
        log.info(
            "pipeline_succeeded",
            total_duration_ms=total_ms,
            total_records=len(transformed),
        )
    except Exception:
        total_ms = round((time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000, 2)
        log.error("pipeline_failed", total_duration_ms=total_ms, exc_info=True)
        raise

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_pipeline("orders", "orders_warehouse")
Sample output (one line per event, formatted for readability here):
automatically from context variables, plus stage-specific fields from binding.

A production e-commerce ETL pipeline runs every hour, extracting from 12 source tables, applying business logic transformations, and loading into a data warehouse. The pipeline runs in Celery workers on Kubernetes, with logs shipped to Elasticsearch via Filebeat.
A Grafana dashboard showing average stage latency per pipeline over 7 days, alerting if
that on-call engineers check first when an alert fires.
event — an alert fires if it exceeds 0.001 (0.1%) for any run, catching upstream data quality issues automatically.
, an engineer can retrieve every log event from that run in chronological order, seeing exactly what happened, how long each stage took, and which records failed.
is also passed as a header to any downstream API calls and included in database audit log entries, making cross-system tracing trivial.
Logging inside tight inner loops without sampling.
Logging every record in a 10-million-row pipeline can generate gigabytes of logs per run and add seconds of CPU overhead from JSON serialization. Log summaries and periodic progress events instead.
or string concatenation in log messages.
constructs the string even if the DEBUG level is disabled. Use
— structlog evaluates fields lazily and only serializes if the level passes.
block that silently discards failures is the enemy of observability. Always log the exception with
in another breaks queries and dashboards. Define a central vocabulary of field names and enforce it across the team. A
PII, passwords, API keys, and raw database records must never appear in logs. Always log identifiers (
. Configuration is the application's responsibility. Library code should only call
and let the application decide how logs are handled.
Not logging at the right level.
for everything makes it impossible to filter signal from noise. Use
for handled anomalies (skipped records, retried operations),
for failures that affect output, and
for failures that stop the pipeline entirely.

Every pipeline execution should generate a UUID at startup and bind it to all loggers via context variables. This is the single most important field for debugging — it groups all events from one run together regardless of which worker or log file they land in.
The event name should be a snake_case noun phrase describing what happened:
. Event names are categorical keys you'll use in queries and alerts.
All logging configuration (handlers, formatters, log level) lives in one place, called once at application startup. Business logic modules just call
to narrow context as you go deeper.
Start with pipeline-level fields at the top, add stage-level fields when entering a stage, add batch-level fields when processing a batch. Never pass the same field to every log call manually.
Emit both start and end events for stages.
event lets you detect hung stages (events with no matching
gives you latency tracking. Both together give you a complete picture.
If your pipeline always works with a
object, write a processor that extracts
is passed as a field value. This avoids repetitive manual field extraction throughout the codebase.

def process_dataset(logger, method, event_dict):
    """Auto-expand Dataset objects into log fields."""
    dataset = event_dict.pop("dataset", None)
    if dataset is not None:
        event_dict["dataset_name"] = dataset.name
        event_dict["dataset_rows"] = dataset.row_count
        event_dict["dataset_source"] = dataset.source
    return event_dict
At low to moderate pipeline volumes (up to ~100K records per run), the overhead of structured logging is negligible. At very high volumes, a few techniques matter:

— structlog's default is to re-evaluate the entire processor chain configuration on every log call. With this flag, the chain is compiled once per logger on first use, eliminating repeated introspection overhead. Always enable this in production.
When logging inside loops, check the level first:
Standard file handlers use blocking I/O. In async pipelines, use a queue-based handler that writes log records to an in-memory queue, with a background thread flushing to disk or a network sink.

import logging
import logging.handlers
import queue

log_queue = queue.Queue(-1)  # Unlimited size
queue_handler = logging.handlers.QueueHandler(log_queue)
stream_handler = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
listener = logging.handlers.QueueListener(log_queue, stream_handler, respect_handler_level=True)
listener.start()
# Attach queue_handler to root logger; all logging is now non-blocking

In containerized production environments, write logs to stdout only and let the infrastructure (Fluentd, Filebeat, the Docker/Kubernetes log driver) handle delivery to the log store. This decouples log durability from your process's I/O and prevents disk-full failures from crashing your pipeline.
— The gold standard for structured logging in Python. Provides a composable processor chain, context binding,
integration, and excellent async support. The first choice for any new project.
— A lightweight alternative that adds a JSON formatter to the stdlib
module. Less powerful than structlog but useful if you need minimal dependencies.
— A user-friendly logging library with a simpler API. Its structured output support is less mature than structlog, but its async support and lazy field evaluation are excellent for smaller projects.

— OpenTelemetry provides a unified standard for logs, metrics, and distributed traces. Particularly important when pipelines call external services or span multiple microservices. Log events can be correlated with distributed traces via
— The classic stack. Filebeat or Logstash ships JSON log files to Elasticsearch, where they become searchable structured documents.
— Log aggregation optimized for Kubernetes, designed to work alongside Prometheus metrics. Uses labels (like Prometheus) for indexing rather than full-text search.
AWS CloudWatch Logs / Google Cloud Logging / Azure Monitor
— Native cloud log services that accept JSON log lines from stdout in containerized workloads with zero configuration.
— Full SaaS observability platform with log parsing, dashboards, and alerting tightly integrated.
Never log credentials, tokens, or PII.

This is the most critical security concern. Database connection strings, API keys, and user personal data that appear in logs become security vulnerabilities if logs are stored, shipped, or accessed by unauthorized parties. Write a structlog processor that scrubs or masks known sensitive field names before serialization.

If user-controlled data appears in log fields, a malicious user could inject fake log entries by embedding newlines and JSON in their input. Structured JSON logging with a proper serializer largely eliminates this risk compared to text logging, because field values are safely escaped inside the JSON string.
If your log handler blocks (e.g., on a slow network write to a remote log system) and your pipeline waits for it, a log infrastructure failure will take down your pipeline. Always use the
pattern in production, which makes logging asynchronous and ensures a log system outage degrades log visibility but never causes pipeline failures.

High-volume pipelines can generate significant log data. Define retention policies per log level — DEBUG logs might be retained for 24 hours, INFO for 30 days, ERROR and above for 1 year. This controls storage costs while ensuring audit trails are preserved.
Structured logging means emitting machine-readable JSON events with named fields, not human-readable sentences. This single change makes your logs queryable, alertable, and aggregatable.
Every pipeline run should generate a unique
, bound to all loggers via context variables so every event from that run is automatically tagged and traceable.
integration make structured logging ergonomic and powerful without boilerplate.
Log at stage boundaries with start/end events and
— this gives you latency tracking, stuck-stage detection, and performance regression visibility with zero additional work.
Never log inside tight inner loops without sampling. Log summaries and periodic progress events instead, using per-record logging only at DEBUG level when explicitly enabled.
Keep all logging configuration in one place at application startup. Library code should only get loggers and use them — never configure handlers or formatters.
In production containers, write logs to stdout only and let the infrastructure handle delivery. The
pattern ensures log I/O never blocks your pipeline even under load.
